fr:mainmatter.fr:blockquote: My research interests lie mainly in type theory, proof assistants, homotopy theory and constructive mathematics.

fr:title.text: Jon Sterling › students › PhD thesis committees

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Jon Sterling › students › PhD thesis committees

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: I currently serve on the following PhD committees:

fr:ul.fr:li: Wojciech Nawrocki: 2024–present, Kripke-Joyal Forcing as a Modality in Lean 4.

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: I was internal examiner for the following PhD students at University of Cambridge:

fr:ul.fr:li: Alex Rice: 2024, A type-theoretic approach to semistrict higher categories.

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: I was external examiner/committee member for the following PhD students:

fr:ul.fr:li: Yue Niu: 2024, Cost-sensitive programming, verification, and semantics.
Loïc Pujet: 2022, Computing with Extensionality Principles in Type Theory.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Controlling unfolding in type theory
fr:frontmatter.fr:meta: Workshop on Dependent Type Theory (to celebrate the Defense of Loïc Pujet)

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: We present a novel mechanism for controlling the unfolding of definitions in dependent type theory. Traditionally, proof assistants let users specify whether each definition can or cannot be unfolded in the remainder of a development; unfolding definitions is often necessary in order to reason about them, but an excess of unfolding can result in brittle proofs and intractably large proof goals. In our system, definitions are by default not unfolded, but users can selectively unfold them in a local manner. We justify our mechanism by means of elaboration to a core type theory with extension types, a connective first introduced in the context of homotopy type theory. We prove a normalization theorem for our core calculus and have implemented our system in the cooltt proof assistant, providing both theoretical and practical evidence for it.

fr:title.text: Computing with Extensionality Principles in Type Theory

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Computing with Extensionality Principles in Type Theory
Cubical synthetic homotopy theory

Proceedings of the 9th ACM SIGPLAN International Conference on Certified Programs and Proofs
